description: Culinary-science grounding for the cooking lens — the food-chemistry and heat-transfer principles behind why techniques work, not just recipes.

entries.title: The Maillard reaction
entries.summary: The Maillard reaction is the non-enzymatic browning between amino acids and reducing sugars that develops hundreds of savory/roasted flavor and aroma compounds. It runs meaningfully above ~140°C/285°F, which is why searing, roasting, and toasting need a dry, hot surface — water caps food at 100°C, so browning only starts once the surface dries. Distinct from caramelization (sugars alone).

entries.title: Caramelization
entries.summary: Caramelization is the thermal breakdown of sugars (starting ~160°C/320°F for sucrose) into nutty, bitter, complex flavor compounds and brown color — no protein required, unlike Maillard. It's behind caramel, the edge of roasted vegetables, and the color of dark syrups; pushed too far it turns acrid and burnt.

entries.title: Emulsification
entries.summary: An emulsion suspends one liquid as droplets in another that normally won't mix (oil in water) using an emulsifier whose molecules bridge both — lecithin (egg yolk) for mayonnaise and hollandaise, mustard for vinaigrette. Stability needs slow oil addition and adequate emulsifier; a 'broken' sauce is the droplets re-coalescing, often rescuable by re-emulsifying into a fresh yolk or water.

entries.title: Protein denaturation & coagulation
entries.summary: Heat (or acid/salt) unfolds proteins (denaturation) and they then bond into a network (coagulation). Eggs set in stages (whites ~62°C, yolks ~68°C), which is why custards curdle if overheated. In meat, myosin sets ~50°C and collagen converts to gelatin only with long cooking above ~70°C — the reason tough cuts need low-and-slow braising while tender cuts want quick high heat.

entries.title: Heat transfer: conduction, convection, radiation
entries.summary: Cooking is applied heat transfer: conduction (direct contact — a pan sear), convection (moving fluid — boiling, a fan oven, frying), and radiation (infrared — broiling, grilling). Water's high heat capacity and the cap at 100°C make boiling gentle and even; oil and dry air go far hotter, enabling browning. Carryover cooking (residual conduction after removal) is why you rest and pull meat early.

entries.title: Fermentation
entries.summary: Fermentation harnesses microbes to transform food: yeast converts sugars to CO2 + alcohol (bread rise, beer), lactic-acid bacteria acidify (sauerkraut, yogurt, sourdough, kimchi), and molds/bacteria ripen cheese and miso. Salt concentration and temperature select for the right microbes and suppress spoilage — the control variables that make fermentation safe and reproducible.

entries.title: Starch gelatinization & thickening
entries.summary: Starch granules absorb water and swell when heated past their gelatinization temperature (~60–80°C), thickening sauces; this is how a roux, slurry, or risotto bodies up. Overheating or over-stirring (especially with high-amylose starch) can break the gel; acid and sugar raise the temperature needed. Retrogradation (re-crystallization on cooling) is why sauces thicken further and bread stales.

entries.title: Salt: seasoning, osmosis & brining
entries.summary: Salt does more than season: by osmosis it draws moisture out (curing, drawing water from vegetables) yet a brine ultimately helps meat retain moisture by dissolving muscle proteins so they hold water and stay juicy. Salt also suppresses spoilage microbes and, added early, penetrates and seasons throughout rather than just the surface.

entries.title: Acids, bases & pH in cooking
entries.summary: pH shapes flavor, color, and texture: acid brightens and balances richness, denatures proteins (ceviche 'cooks' fish, buttermilk tenderizes), and keeps green vegetables' color briefly while prolonged heat dulls them. Baking soda (base) + an acid produce CO2 for leavening (baking powder pairs both); alkalinity also speeds Maillard browning (pretzels, ramen noodles).

entries.title: Food safety: the danger zone & safe temperatures
entries.summary: Bacteria multiply fastest in the 'danger zone' ~5–60°C/40–140°F; keep food below or above it. Safe internal temperatures pasteurize (poultry 74°C/165°F, ground meat 71°C/160°F, whole cuts 63°C/145°F with rest) — pasteurization is time-and-temperature, so lower temps held longer (sous-vide) are equally safe. Cool leftovers quickly through the danger zone and reheat thoroughly.
